name: The Four Kerubim (Tarot Tradition)
description: The Four Kerubim as depicted in Tarot tradition, deriving from the biblical cherubim of Ezekiel and Revelation, representing the four Evangelists and four fixed zodiacal signs.
symbolism: The traditional Sphinx combines all four Kerubim: the body of a bull, the paws of a lion, the wings of an eagle, and the head of a human. This composite creature represents the integration of all four elements and the mastery required to solve the riddle of existence. The Sphinx asks, "What walks on four legs in the morning, two at noon, and three in the evening?" The answer— humanity—points to the human (Aquarius) as the synthesizing principle that understands all stages of manifestation.
behavior: Also known as 🦁🦅🐂👼 The Four Kerubim.

christian.overview: The Four Kerubim as they appear in Tarot tradition (particularly the World and Wheel of Fortune cards) derive directly from biblical cherubim, especially Ezekiel 1 and Revelation 4. This represents Christianity's enduring influence on Western esoteric symbolism.

biblicalOrigin.ezekielVision: Ezekiel 1:5-14 describes the four living creatures with faces of man, lion, ox, and eagle - the direct source for Tarot kerubim iconography.
biblicalOrigin.revelationContinuity: Revelation 4:6-8 continues the tradition, placing the four living creatures around God's throne in perpetual worship.
biblicalOrigin.gospelConnection: The Church's association of these four faces with the four Evangelists (Matthew-Man, Mark-Lion, Luke-Ox, John-Eagle) provides the theological framework adopted by Tarot.

tarotDevelopment.historicalContext: Tarot emerged in 15th century Christian Europe and incorporated traditional Christian symbolism, including the cherubim.
tarotDevelopment.worldCard: The World (XXI) typically shows the dancing figure surrounded by the four kerubim, representing cosmic completion and the four fixed signs of the zodiac as well as the four Gospels.
tarotDevelopment.wheelOfFortune: The Wheel of Fortune (X) often includes the four kerubim reading books (the Gospels), representing eternal wisdom overseeing temporal change.

christianEvaluation.legitimateRoots: The kerubim imagery in Tarot preserves authentic Christian symbolism, even when used in non-Christian contexts.
christianEvaluation.theologicalCaution: While the imagery is biblically grounded, its use in divination diverges from the creatures' biblical function as worshippers and guardians.
christianEvaluation.reclamationPotential: Some Christian interpreters see in Tarot kerubim an opportunity to reclaim profound biblical symbols for their original purpose.

evangelistCorrespondence.lion: Mark - Christ's royal power and resurrection
evangelistCorrespondence.eagle: John - Christ's divine nature and ascension

christian.typologicalReading: The four living creatures represent the fullness of creation worshipping its Creator. Their presence in both biblical vision and Tarot imagery witnesses to the enduring power of Christian symbolism in Western culture.
scholarlyReferences: Decker, Ronald, Thierry Depaulis, and Michael Dummett. A Wicked Pack of Cards: Origins of the Occult Tarot. New York: St. Martin's Press, 1996.
Place, Robert M. The Tarot: History, Symbolism, and Divination. New York: Tarcher, 2005.
Block, Daniel I. The Book of Ezekiel. Grand Rapids: Eerdmans, 1997.

keyMyths.title: The Vision of Ezekiel
keyMyths.description: Ezekiel's vision describes four living creatures, each with four faces (man, lion, ox, eagle) and four wings, forming the basis for the Kerubim imagery. These creatures guard the throne of God and represent divine power and mobility.

keyMyths.title: Revelation's Living Creatures
keyMyths.description: The Book of Revelation echoes Ezekiel's vision, depicting four living creatures around God's throne, each resembling a different animal: a lion, an ox, a man, and an eagle. They constantly praise God and symbolize different aspects of creation and divine attributes.

keyMyths.title: Attribution to the Evangelists
keyMyths.description: Christian tradition associates the four living creatures with the four Evangelists: Matthew (man/angel), Mark (lion), Luke (ox), and John (eagle). Each Evangelist's Gospel is seen to embody the characteristics represented by their corresponding creature.

keyMyths.title: Zodiacal Correspondences
keyMyths.description: In Tarot and esoteric traditions, the Kerubim are linked to the four fixed signs of the zodiac: Aquarius (man), Leo (lion), Taurus (ox), and Scorpio (eagle, often replaced by serpent). These associations connect the Kerubim to elemental forces and the cyclical nature of time.

keyMyths.title: Guardians of the World
keyMyths.description: Within some occult interpretations, the Four Kerubim serve as the guardians of the four corners of the world, ensuring balance and protecting the earthly realm. They are invoked in rituals to establish sacred space and maintain cosmic order.

sources.name: The Holy Bible
sources.description: Primary source for the initial descriptions of the cherubim in Ezekiel and Revelation.
sources.title: The Holy Bible

sources.name: A Dictionary of Symbols
sources.description: A comprehensive reference for symbolic meanings, including the cherubim and their associated elements.
sources.title: A Dictionary of Symbols

sources.name: Meditations on the Tarot: A Journey into Christian Hermeticism
sources.description: An in-depth exploration of the Tarot archetypes, including the Kerubim and their symbolic significance.
sources.title: Meditations on the Tarot: A Journey into Christian Hermeticism

sources.name: The Pictorial Key to the Tarot
sources.description: A foundational text in Tarot studies, providing interpretations and symbolic meanings of the Tarot cards, including those associated with the Kerubim.
sources.title: The Pictorial Key to the Tarot

sources.name: The Kabbalah Unveiled
sources.description: A translation and commentary on sections of the Zohar, exploring the Kabbalistic roots of the Kerubim symbolism.
sources.title: The Kabbalah Unveiled

sources.name: The Complete Golden Dawn System of Magic
sources.description: A compilation of rituals and teachings from the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn, detailing the use of the Kerubim in magical practice.
sources.title: The Complete Golden Dawn System of Magic
